title: Why the return of feeling safe in your body changes everything
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The feeling I had almost forgotten

I was standing in my kitchen, making tea. Nothing special. Just a Tuesday afternoon. And then it hit me. A sensation I couldn't name at first. My shoulders dropped. My jaw unclenched. My breath went deep, slow, easy. I felt my feet on the floor. Not just aware of them, but actually feeling the ground holding me up. It lasted maybe thirty seconds before my brain caught up and started asking questions. What was that? Where did it come from? And the bigger question, the one that made my chest tighten: when was the last time I felt this way?

I couldn't remember. That's the truth. I could remember panic attacks, hypervigilance, the constant scanning of rooms for exits and threats. I could remember the knot in my stomach that lived there for years, the way I'd hold my breath without knowing it, the chronic tension in my neck and shoulders that felt like just part of who I was. But safety? In my body? That was a foreign country I'd heard rumors about but never visited. Here's the thing. When you've lived in survival mode for so long, you don't even know what you're missing. You think the constant alertness is normal. You think the exhaustion is just life. You think the disconnection from your body is just how adults feel.

Then one day, for no obvious reason, your nervous system drops its guard for a moment. And you realize you've been living in a war zone that nobody else could see.

That moment in my kitchen changed things. Not because it fixed everything. It didn't. But because it showed me what was possible. It gave me a target. A direction. A felt sense of what I was actually working toward. And that, more than any insight or theory, is what started the real shift.

What safety actually is, and what it isn't

Let me be clear about something. Feeling safe in your body is not the same as being in a safe situation. You can be in the safest room on earth, with locks on the doors and people who love you nearby, and still feel like you're about to be attacked. That's the legacy of trauma. Your nervous system learned a pattern, and it stuck. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, calls this neuroception. It's your nervous system's automatic, unconscious scanning for safety and danger. And here's the cruel part. Once your system has learned that the world is dangerous, it keeps finding evidence to confirm that belief. Even when the danger is long gone.

So safety isn't about circumstances. It's not about having a good job or a loving partner or a safe neighborhood. Those things can help, sure. But if your nervous system is still stuck in threat mode, none of it matters. You'll find the threat anyway. You'll create it if you have to. Because your system's job is to keep you alive, not to make you feel good. And survival mode doesn't care about your quality of life.

I spent years trying to think my way out of this. I read books. I journaled. I analyzed my childhood, my relationships, my patterns. I could tell you exactly why I was the way I was. But knowing didn't change the feeling. My body didn't care about my insights. It cared about safety, and it didn't feel any. That's the gap that so much of the wellness industry misses. They talk about healing like it's a cognitive process. Like if you just understand your trauma enough, you'll be free. But the body doesn't speak the language of understanding. It speaks the language of sensation, of rhythm, of felt experience. You can't talk your nervous system into feeling safe. You have to show it, again and again, that safety is real.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker does an incredible job of naming the specific patterns that come with chronic relational trauma, and that naming alone can be a form of relief. But even he would tell you that the real work happens in the body.

What changes when your body finally believes it's safe

I'm going to tell you what changed for me, but I want you to know that your experience might look different. That's okay. The specifics matter less than the direction.

First, my thinking changed. Not because I tried to think differently, but because my brain had more resources available. When you're in survival mode, your prefrontal cortex goes offline. That's the part of your brain that handles complex reasoning, impulse control, future planning. It's expensive to run, and your body doesn't want to waste energy on it when there's a tiger nearby. So when my nervous system started to settle, I suddenly had access to parts of my mind that had been locked away. I could pause before reacting. I could see more options. I could hold two conflicting feelings without needing to resolve them immediately. That was huge.

Second, my relationships changed. This one surprised me. I thought I was a pretty good friend, partner, family member. But I was always operating from a place of subtle defensiveness. I was waiting for the other shoe to drop. I was scanning for signs of rejection or betrayal. And that scanning showed up in my body language, my tone, my availability. People could feel it, even if they couldn't name it. When I started to feel safer in my own body, I could actually be present with other people. I could listen without preparing my response. I could receive care without wondering what they wanted from me. I could let people in.

Third, my capacity for joy changed. This one wrecked me, honestly. I didn't know I was missing it. I thought I felt joy. I felt pleasure, satisfaction, amusement. But real joy, the kind that makes you laugh until you cry or cry until you laugh, the kind that feels like your heart might burst open. That requires a nervous system that feels safe enough to open. And mine didn't. Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body as incomplete survival responses. The energy of fight, flight, or freeze gets stuck. And that stuck energy blocks not just pain but also pleasure. When you release the trauma, you don't just feel less bad. You feel more good. That was the part I wasn't ready for.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. Her work on the RAIN practice helped me learn to be with my experience without trying to fix it. And that capacity to be with, rather than fix, turned out to be essential for building safety.

The slow, unglamorous work of building safety

Here's what nobody tells you. Building safety in your body is boring. It's slow. It's repetitive. It's not dramatic or cathartic. It's not the kind of thing that makes for a good story. It's sitting on a cushion and noticing that your jaw is clenched for the hundredth time. It's taking a breath and feeling your feet on the floor for the thousandth time. It's noticing a flash of panic and staying in the room instead of dissociating or distracting. It's doing this over and over and over, for months and years, until your nervous system starts to get the message. This is safe. You can rest now.

I used to think healing was about the big breakthroughs. The crying that goes on for hours. The memory that finally surfaces. The moment of forgiveness or release. And those things happen sometimes. They matter. But they're not the foundation. The foundation is the boring, daily practice of coming back to your body and letting it know it's okay. That's what actually changes your nervous system. That's what builds the capacity for safety.

Janina Fisher, who writes about trauma and the body, talks about how our parts or selves can be stuck in different trauma responses. One part might be frozen in childhood, another part might be fighting, another part might be fleeing. And these parts don't trust that the present is safe. They're still living in the past. So building safety means communicating with those parts, showing them evidence that the danger is over. And that communication isn't verbal. It's somatic. It's about tone, rhythm, temperature, pressure. It's about the quality of your attention, not the content of your thoughts.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. His Internal Family Systems model gave me a language for the different voices inside me, and more , a way to relate to them with curiosity instead of shame. That shift from shame to curiosity is itself a form of safety.

What it means when the return happens

I don't want to pretend that feeling safe in your body is a permanent state. It's not. I still have days where my nervous system is on high alert for no reason I can identify. I still have moments where the old patterns kick in and I'm scanning for threats, bracing for impact, holding my breath. But those moments are shorter now. They don't define my whole day. And more , I know what safety feels like. I have a reference point. So when I lose it, I know it's temporary. I know how to find my way back.

That's what the return changes. Not that you never feel unsafe again. But that you know what you're returning to. You have a home base. A place in your body that you can come back to, again and again, no matter how far you wander. And that changes everything. It changes how you relate to your own experience. It changes how you show up in relationships. It changes what you believe is possible for yourself.

I used to think healing was about getting rid of my trauma. Now I think it's about building enough safety in my body that the trauma can be held. That the pain can be witnessed without overwhelming the system. That the fear can be felt without taking over. That's a different goal entirely. And it's one that I actually have some control over.

So if you're reading this and you don't know what safety feels like in your body, I want you to know that it's possible. Not guaranteed, not easy, not quick. But possible. The path is different for everyone. For some people it's yoga or martial arts or dance. For others it's therapy or massage or time in nature. For me it was a combination of things, including a lot of sitting still and noticing what I was feeling without trying to change it. A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. That question, what happened to you instead of what's wrong with you, opened a door for me that I didn't even know was closed.

The return of feeling safe in your body changes everything because it changes the ground you stand on. It changes the foundation of your entire experience. And from that new ground, everything else becomes possible. Not easy. But possible. And that, honestly, is enough.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey's compassionate reframing of trauma
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's guide for survivors of chronic relational trauma
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz's introduction to Internal Family Systems
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - Tara Brach's work on embracing your experience
